And again, I think that the big criticisms that somehow it's a lower quality manager just don't hold up under the scrutiny that I just shared. And you can go to the next slide. And really, as a final takeaway here, I would just add this isn't just my opinion. Insiders are putting their money where their mouth is on this.

So senior management gets all their equity there. It gets all their compensation in equity, Blue Owl stock. And so they're already heavily lined with shareholders without having to buy any of themselves. But that being said, they still purchased over $15 million worth of shares just this past November and December at a stock price that's very similar to where the stock is trading today. $15 million, that's not a little bit.

Not only that, but the company itself repurchased more than $50 million worth of the stock under its buyback program. On top of that, the subsidiaries, the publicly traded BDCs, OBDC and OTF that I mentioned earlier, they repurchased approximately $115 million worth of their own stock on a combined basis during that same timeframe, and Insiders in those 2 companies purchased more than $20 million of stock.

So again, there's clearly a lot of confidence in their private credit business. There's clearly a lot of confidence in OWL as well on the insider basis. So when you combine all that with the strong credit rating, the significant liquidity, the growth track record, the asset light fee-based earnings business model with the high yield and the attractive valuations compared to peers with, again, just that misunderstanding about the 2 big risks that they're getting and all the negative headlines.

I think this is one of those asymmetric bets that is currently hated, but I think for patient investors who can ride the negative headlines and the volatility that comes with it are going to be richly rewarded over time.

RS: Samuel, thank you for that very much. Really appreciate that take.

Steven and Samuel, I wanted to give you each a chance to reflect on each other's presentation or things that you may have forgotten to bring up or things of value to note for investors.

Steven, I'll start with you.

SB: Yes. Samuel's presentation was terrific. He's a real analyst. I'm a portfolio manager and generalist, but he really delved into it.

I was checking out Blue Owl, and one of my favorite funds is (PBDC). It's an ETF. It's the Putnam BDC, and it's an actively managed BDC fund. And I was looking at it while he was talking and noticed that 2 out of its 3 top holdings, I think, were Blue Owl entities.

One was the Blue Owl BDC, and then the other one was another Blue Owl. You probably know more about it, Samuel. But anyway, Blue Owl was right at the top of their list, which I thought sort of helped your case, not that you needed it.

RS: Samuel, thoughts?

SS: No, I hadn't noticed that, but I see it now. Yeah, they had the Blue Owl technology fund, which is their second largest holding and then tied for third is the OBDC, the Blue Owl Capital Corp. And it's nice to see that confirmation. I mean, I agree with them.

I actually - full disclosure, I just bought some more shares of OTF this morning, we hold that as well as OWL, at High Yield Investor, and I'm very bullish on their, their, their technology, their software lending business as well. But yeah, no, that's great to hear.

And as far as your presentation, I can definitely see the appeal that approach for an investor who, again, doesn't want to have to deal with digging into individual stocks and wants to just have a portfolio that pays the cash.

And I completely agree with your emphasis on the income component, especially for someone, you know, in retirement who's not trying to set records. They're not necessarily trying to beat the market. They simply, they're more concerned about sequence of returns risk, rather than absolute total returns.

I think of an analogy that Howard Marks, you're probably familiar with him. He's a famous credit investor, speaking of direct lending and some of the funds you invest in are probably credit funds based on the book that I read that you've written.

He said there's a story about a man who was 6 feet tall, who drowned while trying to cross a river that was only 4 feet deep on average. And that's because there may be a period that's, if he doesn't know how to swim and say, there's like a 20, 30 foot span in that river, that's, you know, 14, 20 feet deep.

He's going to drown. And that same goes for a portfolio that, yeah, maybe the long-term return from the market is 10%.

But if you go through a market crash followed by a lost decade, which we certainly could encounter, and he's also recently pointed out that where the S&P 500 is currently priced, historically, it's always delivered around 0% average annualized returns in the decade following, whenever it's reached this level.

And we see what's going on in the world as Rena was mentioning at the beginning that wouldn't shock. I'm not making that prediction, but it wouldn't shock me at all if that happened.

And so if you're an income investor instead, especially if you're invested in credit, you know, contractual returns from debt investments and you're diversified and you have some quality managers, which I believe Blue Owl is one of them, even if you have a few credit losses, you're still going to probably get a high single digits annualized return that's consistently being paid to you via those contractual payments and you'll survive your retirement.

And so again, as Warren Buffett once said, it's insanity to risk what you need and have, namely your principal and that steady stream of income in retirement in order to risk that in order to get something that you don't have and you don't need.

If you're a retiree and you have a good nest egg, you don't need a 20% annualized return. Hopefully, you don't need a 15% annualized return to meet your needs in retirement.

And so it's better to hedge your bets and sign up for a more secure 7 to 10 percent return that you get consistently than go for the volatile ride and put yourself at risk for coming up short.

So anyway, I really appreciate that component of your strategy. And I try to implement something similar in my retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor, albeit with some different vehicles than you use. But I think you can get a similar outcome.

RS: I would say patience and common sense still at a premium during these times, maybe more than ever, but still really, really important to hold onto those 2 things. Steven, any further thoughts before we get to Q&A?

SB: Well, yes. Again, I think Samuel and I are really on a lot of the same page, even though we have somewhat different strategies that may reflect various differences in where we are in our lives and our personal investing careers.

If you go back before 1980 or so, most people had jobs where they had pensions as opposed. Now we have to create our own pension, so to speak, through our 401Ks and our IRAs.

But in the old days, if you will, when people had defined benefit pensions, they looked forward to retiring on a pension, and they didn't even think about how many assets the pension company had to have in order to pay me X amount per month.

When that changed, it got us as retail investors thinking a lot more about how to do the investing ourselves. But it's that income that you're, to me, it's the income that provides the source of economic value.

In fact, the famous economists have written that it's the market price of something is the discounted current value of all of its future cash flows.

And if you think about the value coming from the income you're going to get, it makes it easier to forget about short-term market movements and focus on that income.

RS: Thank you, Samuel. Thank you, Steven. Really appreciate you both.

Steven's investing group is called Inside the Income Factory. Samuel's is called High Yield Investor.

data.title: MSCI: Simply A Solid Investment
data.description:
One and a half years ago, I published my last article about MSCI Inc. (MSCI). And after I had been rather bearish in previous articles about the stock, I saw the picture improving as the stock price stagnated more or less while the fundamental picture still improved. I wrote in my conclusion back then:

At this point, we can argue that MSCI is fairly valued. When taking analysts’ growth estimates and the company’s own optimistic long-term targets, the stock is trading for a reasonable price.

But I would still be rather cautious at this point. When considering the risks mentioned above and the steep decline of free cash flow following the Great Financial Crisis, I can easily imagine the stock declining lower. We should also keep in mind that the stock traded for valuation multiples in the low teens following the years of the Great Financial Crisis – and this could happen again.

In my last article, I switched the rating to “Hold” after I had a “Sell” rating on the stock in the previous articles. And in this article, I will rate the stock as a “Hold” once again. The stock is not so overvalued anymore to rate it with a “Sell” but it is far from being a bargain or a great investment. In my opinion, we are still faced with downside risks at this point.

Since my last article was published, MSCI has increased about 24% in value and 26.5% when including dividends (the stock performed almost in line with the S&P 500 Index (SP500)).

In the following article, I will argue once again that MSCI is fairly valued or maybe a bit undervalued. I will once again argue that MSCI still has solid growth potential, but that growth potential is almost priced into the stock at this point and therefore investors should not expect more than 10% annual return on investment (which is a solid return). We will look at different growth opportunities in the years to come (including share buybacks) but start with the last quarterly results.

Quarterly Results

Today, on January 28, 2026, MSCI reported its fourth quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2025. While the company did beat a little bit on earnings per share estimates for the fourth quarter, revenue was almost in line (a very narrow miss). Operating revenue increased from $743.5 million in Q4/24 to $822.5 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 10.6% year-over-year growth. Organic growth was 10.2% in the fourth quarter. Operating income also increased 14.4% year-over-year from $405.2 million in the same quarter last year to $463.6 million in this quarter.

Finally, diluted earnings per share decreased from $3.90 in Q4/24 to $3.81 in Q4/25. However, when looking at adjusted earnings per share we see an increase of 11.5% year-over-year from $4.18 in the same quarter last year to $4.66 in this quarter. Adjusted EBITDA increased from 60.8% in Q4/24 to 62.2% in Q4/25.

Looking at the full year results, the picture is similar. Operating revenue increased from $2,856 million in fiscal 2024 to $3,134 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 10.5% year-over-year growth. Operating income also increased from $1,529 million in the previous year to $1,714 million in this year – resulting in 12.1% year-over-year growth. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $14.05 in fiscal 2024 to $15.69 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 11.7% year-over-year growth.

MSCI is reporting in four different segments, and all four segments contributed to year-over-year growth and while the Index segment reported 16% organic growth for its run rate, the other three segments reported growth rates around 10%.

Growth Opportunities Ahead

MSCI is mostly a subscription-based business, and these subscriptions lead to high levels of stability for growth over the years. Not only is the quarterly retention rate between 92.6% and 96.4% in every single quarter since Q4/20, but the organic subscription run rate growth is very stable over time. The number is fluctuating between 7% and 14%, but over the long run we can be quite confident in expecting growth rates in the high single digits (or maybe even low double digits).

And for fiscal 2026, management is also slightly optimistic. However, the guidance is missing key metrics like revenue and earnings per share, and we must calculate several metrics ourselves. However, adjusted operating expenses will be about 5% to 8% higher and interest expenses are expected to jump about 30%. In the end, we should be rather cautious about double-digit bottom line growth next year.

Management is issuing guidance for one important financial metric – the free cash flow. In fiscal 2026, it is expected to be in a range of $1,470 million to $1,530 million and therefore only about 1% to 5% higher compared to fiscal 2025.

Although expectations for fiscal 2026 are rather low, management is expecting to profit from a long-term shift in investing styles. During the UBS Global Technology and AI Conference, the CFO Andrew Wiechmann commented:

And if you look at the biggest trends taking place within investing, you're seeing a move towards rules based or systematic, if you will, customized portfolios and customized solutions.

That is one of the biggest trends you are seeing across the investment industry, all parts of the investment industry. That's manifesting itself in things like multi-asset class solutions portfolios, model portfolios, direct index portfolios, structured products, basket trades, all those are some manifestation of that more systematic personalized type of investment strategy.“

And aside from these trends in investing towards a more systematic and rule-based investment, MSCI is also seeing growth opportunities in areas where MSCI has historically been rather small and did not really have a presence:

And you need these frameworks to span all asset classes. And we are unique in having everything from benchmarks, but through to risk models, risk frameworks and risk management tools across all asset classes. And so we are uniquely positioned to help drive this transformation towards customization. And so the exciting thing for us is not only does that mean we can do more for asset managers that are pivoting their business model, and that's our largest client segment, but we are seeing tremendous opportunities in client segments where we've historically been small and that's areas like wealth management, areas like broker-dealers, hedge funds, the trading community more broadly, insurance companies, corporates directly. These are all outsized growth areas for us.

In the last investor presentation, MSCI also pointed towards several emerging growth opportunities for the business.

MSCI is without much doubt a business that is still growing at a high pace and these companies are often using free cash flow to finance organic growth. Typically, these companies neither pay a dividend nor do they spend huge amounts on share buybacks. MSCI is not only paying a dividend but is also spending huge amounts on share buybacks.

Since 2012, the company spent about $8.1 billion on share buybacks and repurchased shares with a rather aggressive pace. Between 2012 and late 2025, the number of diluted shares outstanding decreased at a CAGR of 3.6% - certainly contributing to earnings per share growth in these 13 years.

I don’t know whether share buybacks were the best strategy and the best way to allocate cash as MSCI certainly was expensive during the last decade. Unless management can’t find better ways to spend cash – acquisitions or organic growth – share buybacks might be the best way and with valuation multiples slowly declining these buybacks are actually getting more effective. And management will most likely continue to repurchase shares with an aggressive pace as a new $3 billion share repurchase program was announced at the end of October 2025. Considering a market capitalization of $44 billion, about 6.8% of shares outstanding can be bought back.

Overall, analysts also remain very optimistic about MSCI and high growth rates in the years to come. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2029, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 12.78% and when comparing this to the growth rates of the last ten years, the assumptions seem rather moderate as earnings per share grew with a CAGR of 19% between fiscal 2015 and fiscal 2025.

Not only analysts are optimistic, but management is optimistic as well. Long-term growth targets still expect the business to grow its revenue in the low double digits and adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow in the low to mid-teens.

Balance Sheet

On small problem I see for the business is the balance sheet. In its investor presentation, MSCI is calling it a strong balance sheet that is providing optionality – I would disagree. A strong balance sheet looks different and the credit ratings by Moody’s; S&P as well as Fitch are also telling a different story.

On December 31, 2025, MSCI is reporting long-term debt of $6,202 million and even when subtracting the total cash of $515 million, net debt of $5,687 million remains. When comparing the total debt to an expected free cash flow of $1.5 billion for fiscal 2026, it would take almost 4 years to repay the outstanding debt. Of course, this is still acceptable for a company growing with a high pace.

Additionally, MSCI has $2,923 million in goodwill on its balance sheet, and therefore more than 50% of its total assets are goodwill and MSCI is reporting a total shareholder’s deficit for several years now. The reasons are the growing amount of treasury stock as MSCI is keeping the repurchased shares on its balance sheet. None of these items, goodwill, the negative shareholder’s equity due to treasury stocks nor the debt levels are a major risk – but a great balance sheet looks different.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In the end, the decisive question remains whether MSCI is now a good investment or whether it is still a Hold or maybe even overvalued again. At the time of writing, MSCI is trading for 33 times free cash flow and 37.4 times earnings. On the one hand, we can make the argument that MSCI is trading below its 10-year average numbers for the P/E ratio and P/FCF ratio and might therefore be trading below its intrinsic value. On the other hand, we should not ignore that a valuation multiple of 30 to 35 is still expensive and can only be justified by high and consistent growth rates.

To also include growth expectations, we can use a discount cash flow calculation once again to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. And as always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve at least for any investment) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which was 74.7 million). As basis we could use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $1,388 million and in the next ten years let’s calculate with a growth rate of 12%, which is more or less in line with analysts’ assumptions.

In ten years from now, we assume 6% growth till perpetuity – a number that can be justified due to the wide economic moat around the business (and the ambitious long-term growth targets management is usually expressing).

When calculating with these optimistic assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $709.87 for MSCI, and the stock can be seen as a bit undervalued and maybe a good investment.

However, we can make the argument to be a little more cautious and not calculate with the free cash flow of the last four quarters. MSCI reported a free cash flow margin of 45.4% recently, which is not only above the long-term average of 34.5% but also above the 10-year average of 40.4%. We certainly can make an argument for an improving margin over time, but the 10-year average seems like a solid basis at this point and would result in a free cash flow around $1,240 million we can assume as basis in our calculation. When calculating with these numbers instead (all other assumptions being the same), we get an intrinsic value of $634.18 for MSCI and the stock would still be slightly undervalued at this point, but certainly not a bargain or a stock one has to buy.

Conclusion

In my opinion, MSCI remains a high-quality business with solid growth opportunities and the ability to grow its bottom line in the high single digits or maybe even low double digits. However, that growth potential is also reflected by the stock price at this point and therefore MSCI remains a Hold in my opinion. This does not mean that MSCI can’t be bought as long-term investment – according to my intrinsic value calculation, fairly valued is implying an annual return around 10%, which is more than solid.
